Lamprophyres are a rare rock-type that include different lithologies, classified mineralogically (abundance of mica, amphibole, feldspathoids) rather than geochemically (enrichment in alkalis and volatiles). Despite the attention that these rocks have attracted, due to their possible economic interest, their sources, petrogenesis and classification remain a matter of debate. One of the most intriguing aspects is the relationship of some lamprophyre sub-types with diamonds, especially because diamondiferous lamprophyres are the rarest diamond-bearing rock-types. The reason for this association and their potential genetic relationship remain poorly understood. Previous studies on lamprophyres (diamond-bearing and barren) are reviewed to improve our knowledge of peculiar mantle paragenesis, fertilization events, metasomatic agents and the relationship between lamprophyres and diamonds. Calc-alkaline lamprophyres (CALs) are often emplaced in subduction-related settings, where slab dehydration and crustal recycling can stabilize hydrous phases (amphibole/phlogopite). Low degree partial melting of shallow metasomatized sources may explain CALs petrogenesis. To reconcile this shallow formation depth with the presence of diamonds, we propose a lithospheric stacking model, that results in a highly transient geotherm during the initial phases of craton formation. Ultramatic lamprophyres (UMLs) mostly crop out along rifts and are related to a veined lithospheric and/or asthenospheric source. A direct genetic link between diamonds and UMLs can be excluded. Instead, the presence of diamondiferous UMLs relies on spatial and temporal connections (e.g., mantle domains enriched in C-H-phases). Lamprophyres are hostile magma for diamonds, due to their high fO2, H2O and CO2, along with a longer lag time and a lower volume of emplaced magma.

item.title: Petrogenesis and tectonomagmatic evolution of diabase dikes in a Tethyan SSZ setting: constraints from U-Pb geochronology, geochemistry and Sr-Nd-Pb isotopes
item.dc:creator: Alper Güneş
Nurdane İlbeyli
Mehmet Demirbilek
Namık Aysal
item.description: Mafic dikes within ophiolitic complexes provide critical constraints on the magmatic and tectonic evolution of supra-subduction zone (SSZ) environments. This study investigates the petrological, geochronological (titanite UPb), mineral-geochemical, and isotopic (Sr-Nd-Pb) characteristics of isolated diabase dikes intruding ultramafic-mafic units in the Tekirova and Gödene Zones of the Tauride Ophiolite within the Eastern Mediterranean Ophiolite Belt. The dikes are dominated by primary plagioclase (labradorite) and clinopyroxene (diopside), with opaque minerals as accessory phases. Amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) and titanite occur both as primary magmatic and secondary phases. Titanite UPb ages from the Tekirova dikes (89.40 ± 13.40 and 80.47 ± 4.77 Ma) constrain Late Cretaceous magmatism. Geochemically, the dikes are enriched in large-ion lithophile elements (LILE) and depleted in high field strength elements (HFSE), displaying pronounced NbTa anomalies consistent with an SSZ arc affinity. Chondrite-normalized rare earth element (REE) patterns show light REE depletion, relatively flat heavy REE distributions, and variable Eu anomalies ((Eu/Eu*) N = 0.21–2.62). Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic compositions (87Sr/86Sr(i) = 0.705455–0.707456; εNd(t) = +4.5 to +8.3; 206Pb/204Pb = 17.89–18.91; 207Pb/204Pb = 15.63–16.31; 208Pb/204Pb = 37.96–39.88) indicate derivation from a depleted N-MORB-like mantle source variably metasomatized by subduction-derived fluids and sediments, with limited crustal contamination. There are compositional differences between the isolated dikes from the Tekirova and Gödene Zones. The Tekirova dikes exhibit closer affinities to a depleted mantle source with limited slab input, whereas the Gödene dikes record stronger metasomatic signatures, including LILE enrichment, pronounced NbTa depletion, and deviation from the mantle array, indicating enhanced involvement of slab-derived components and a more evolved mélange-related setting. Partial melting modeling indicates that the Tekirova dikes derived from a depleted spinel lherzolite source through approximately 1–10% partial melting under shallow upper mantle conditions, whereas the Gödene dikes reflect higher melting degrees (~20%) from the same source, as indicated by >20 LREE systematics. These differences are attributed to mantle source heterogeneity and variable subduction-related contributions. Geothermobarometric estimates suggest shallow crustal crystallization conditions, with Tekirova samples recording slightly higher temperatures and pressures than those from Gödene. In conclusion, the systematic increase in crystallization ages from west to east along the Tauride Ophiolite Belt indicates westward younging of SSZ magmatism. This pattern is interpreted to reflect trench retreat driven by slab roll-back during Neotethys subduction, whereby progressive slab retreat and segmentation of the subduction system induced along-strike (east-west) migration of arc magmatism, resulting in the observed westward shift of magmatic activity. Published: 2026-06-23T23:28:11+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.603 (consider)

Mafic dikes within ophiolitic complexes provide critical constraints on the magmatic and tectonic evolution of supra-subduction zone (SSZ) environments. This study investigates the petrological, geochronological (titanite UPb), mineral-geochemical, and isotopic (Sr-Nd-Pb) characteristics of isolated diabase dikes intruding ultramafic-mafic units in the Tekirova and Gödene Zones of the Tauride Ophiolite within the Eastern Mediterranean Ophiolite Belt. The dikes are dominated by primary plagioclase (labradorite) and clinopyroxene (diopside), with opaque minerals as accessory phases. Amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) and titanite occur both as primary magmatic and secondary phases. Titanite UPb ages from the Tekirova dikes (89.40 ± 13.40 and 80.47 ± 4.77 Ma) constrain Late Cretaceous magmatism. Geochemically, the dikes are enriched in large-ion lithophile elements (LILE) and depleted in high field strength elements (HFSE), displaying pronounced NbTa anomalies consistent with an SSZ arc affinity. Chondrite-normalized rare earth element (REE) patterns show light REE depletion, relatively flat heavy REE distributions, and variable Eu anomalies ((Eu/Eu*) N = 0.21–2.62). Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic compositions (87Sr/86Sr(i) = 0.705455–0.707456; εNd(t) = +4.5 to +8.3; 206Pb/204Pb = 17.89–18.91; 207Pb/204Pb = 15.63–16.31; 208Pb/204Pb = 37.96–39.88) indicate derivation from a depleted N-MORB-like mantle source variably metasomatized by subduction-derived fluids and sediments, with limited crustal contamination. There are compositional differences between the isolated dikes from the Tekirova and Gödene Zones. The Tekirova dikes exhibit closer affinities to a depleted mantle source with limited slab input, whereas the Gödene dikes record stronger metasomatic signatures, including LILE enrichment, pronounced NbTa depletion, and deviation from the mantle array, indicating enhanced involvement of slab-derived components and a more evolved mélange-related setting. These differences are attributed to mantle source heterogeneity and variable subduction-related contributions. In conclusion, the systematic increase in crystallization ages from west to east along the Tauride Ophiolite Belt indicates westward younging of SSZ magmatism. This pattern is interpreted to reflect trench retreat driven by slab roll-back during Neotethys subduction, whereby progressive slab retreat and segmentation of the subduction system induced along-strike (east-west) migration of arc magmatism, resulting in the observed westward shift of magmatic activity.

item.title: 40Ar/39Ar geochronology, SrNd isotopes and melting conditions of the Dahongshan alkaline basalts: Constraints on mantle modification beneath the northern Yangtze Craton
item.dc:creator: Yue Song
Xuan-Ce Wang
Simon A. Wilde
Xiang-Zhong Li
Chong-Jin Pang
Si-Yu Hu
Yang Luo
Jia-Xu Tang
Si-Zhe Fu
item.description: We present new groundmass 40Ar/39Ar ages, whole-rock major and trace element data, SrNd isotopic compositions, and P–T estimates for the Dahongshan alkaline basalts in the northern Yangtze Craton, China. Integrating these data with regional records demonstrates that Late Cretaceous mafic magmatism in the northern Yangtze Craton records a transition from subduction-modified lithospheric mantle to increasingly asthenosphere-influenced melting. Four representative samples yield concordant plateau-like weighted mean 40Ar/39Ar ages of 83.4–84.1 Ma (2σ), constraining the eruption of Dahongshan magmas to ca. 84 Ma. The basalts have intraplate, OIB-like trace element patterns, elevated LREE/HREE ratios, and a narrow range of SrNd isotopic compositions ((87Sr/86Sr)ᵢ = 0.704358–0.704800; εNd(t) = −0.80 to +1.35), indicating derivation from a moderately enriched, pyroxenite-influenced mantle source with a mafic component and only limited syn-ascent crustal assimilation. Low Ce/Pb and Nb/U ratios, together with pronounced positive Pb anomalies, are interpreted as source-inherited features rather than evidence for significant crustal assimilation. Primary melt reconstruction and complementary whole-rock major element modelling yield low- to moderate-pressure P–T estimates of 1.4–2.1 GPa and 1252–1356 °C, interpreted to record final melt–mantle equilibration or segregation during ascent rather than the depth of melt initiation. In contrast, REE systematics indicate a garnet-influenced melting signal, suggesting that melt generation was initiated deeper, probably near the garnet–spinel transition, before undergoing re-equilibration at shallower lithospheric levels. Regional comparisons support a temporal shift from Early Cretaceous lithosphere-derived arc-like magmatism to Late Cretaceous increasingly asthenosphere-influenced volcanism, followed by Cenozoic deeper-sourced alkaline basalts consistent with garnet-facies melting during post-extension thermal adjustment. Published: 2026-06-23T07:13:04+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.603 (consider)

In contrast, REE systematics indicate a garnet-influenced melting signal, suggesting that melt generation was initiated deeper, probably near the garnet–spinel transition, before undergoing re-equilibration at shallower lithospheric levels. item.title: The origin of the post-Marinoan 17O-depleted barite
item.dc:creator: Tingting Chen
Yongbo Peng
Lan-Lan Tian
Nan Sun
Wei Wei
Fang Huang
item.description: Determining the origin of 17O-depleted barite in post-Marinoan cap dolostones is crucial for reconstructing ocean-atmosphere dynamics immediately following Marinoan deglaciation. We investigated coupled δ138Ba, Δ17O, and δ34S in barites from inner-shelf to shelf-lagoon settings in South China to identify barium sources and discriminate between two competing sulfate sources: riverine delivery via continental sulfide oxidation versus local H2S oxidation at a dynamic shelf redoxcline. Results reveal positive covariations among the three isotopes (δ138Ba: −0.08‰ to 0.17‰; Δ17O: −0.83‰ to −0.14‰; δ34S: 22.6‰ to 43.9‰) and systematic spatial gradients. These patterns support barite formation at an oxic-sulfidic redoxcline, where upwelling Ba2+- and H2S-rich deep waters encountered dissolved O2, generating sulfate that inherited the pronounced atmospheric 17O-depletion. Minor nearshore isotopic deviations suggest a subsidiary riverine contribution. This δ138Ba-Δ17O-δ34S isotope system effectively traces sulfate provenance and short-lived redox fluctuations on post-Marinoan shelves, thereby confirming widespread sulfate-depleted, Ba2+- and H2S-rich waters in early Ediacaran oceans. Published: 2026-06-24T07:16:41+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.602 (consider)

Authors: Tingting Chen; Yongbo Peng; Lan-Lan Tian; Nan Sun; Wei Wei; Fang Huang

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120190

Relevance: 0.602 (consider)

Determining the origin of 17O-depleted barite in post-Marinoan cap dolostones is crucial for reconstructing ocean-atmosphere dynamics immediately following Marinoan deglaciation. We investigated coupled δ138Ba, Δ17O, and δ34S in barites from inner-shelf to shelf-lagoon settings in South China to identify barium sources and discriminate between two competing sulfate sources: riverine delivery via continental sulfide oxidation versus local H2S oxidation at a dynamic shelf redoxcline. Results reveal positive covariations among the three isotopes (δ138Ba: −0.08‰ to 0.17‰; Δ17O: −0.83‰ to −0.14‰; δ34S: 22.6‰ to 43.9‰) and systematic spatial gradients. These patterns support barite formation at an oxic-sulfidic redoxcline, where upwelling Ba2+- and H2S-rich deep waters encountered dissolved O2, generating sulfate that inherited the pronounced atmospheric 17O-depletion. Minor nearshore isotopic deviations suggest a subsidiary riverine contribution. This δ138Ba-Δ17O-δ34S isotope system effectively traces sulfate provenance and short-lived redox fluctuations on post-Marinoan shelves, thereby confirming widespread sulfate-depleted, Ba2+- and H2S-rich waters in early Ediacaran oceans.

item.title: The role of plate tectonic-like behaviour in the long-term climate evolution of Earth
item.dc:creator: Takashi Nakagawa
item.description: This study investigates the long-term evolution of Earth’s climate by coupling a simplified atmosphere–ocean model with a carbon cycle that includes the deep interior (deep carbon cycle). Unlike previous studies that relied on parameterised thermal histories, carbon release fluxes here are computed using fully dynamic mantle convection simulations, encompassing mid-ocean ridges, hotspots and subduction-related arc volcanism. Two tectonic regimes are examined: a mobile-lid mode representing plate tectonics and a stagnant-lid mode lacking plate motion. Results indicate that the mobile-lid regime sustains a stable, temperate climate over billions of years by maintaining continuous CO₂ outgassing from the deep mantle. In contrast, the stagnant-lid regime fails to offset the faint young Sun, leaving the planet in a globally frozen state despite increasing insolation. Continental silicate weathering emerges as the dominant carbon sink. These findings highlight the critical role of plate tectonic-like processes in sustaining Earth’s long-term climate stability and habitability and underscore the need for better constraints on deep carbon cycling and its coupling with atmosphere–ocean evolution, given the simplifications and limitations of current models. Published: 2026-06-27T10:56:15+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.601 (consider)

Authors: Takashi Nakagawa

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120193

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

This study investigates the long-term evolution of Earth’s climate by coupling a simplified atmosphere–ocean model with a carbon cycle that includes the deep interior (deep carbon cycle). Unlike previous studies that relied on parameterised thermal histories, carbon release fluxes here are computed using fully dynamic mantle convection simulations, encompassing mid-ocean ridges, hotspots and subduction-related arc volcanism. Two tectonic regimes are examined: a mobile-lid mode representing plate tectonics and a stagnant-lid mode lacking plate motion. Results indicate that the mobile-lid regime sustains a stable, temperate climate over billions of years by maintaining continuous CO₂ outgassing from the deep mantle. In contrast, the stagnant-lid regime fails to offset the faint young Sun, leaving the planet in a globally frozen state despite increasing insolation. Continental silicate weathering emerges as the dominant carbon sink. These findings highlight the critical role of plate tectonic-like processes in sustaining Earth’s long-term climate stability and habitability and underscore the need for better constraints on deep carbon cycling and its coupling with atmosphere–ocean evolution, given the simplifications and limitations of current models.

item.title: Subseafloor processes in ultramafic-influenced volcanic-hosted hydrothermal systems: An insight from the Gnitahei massive sulfide mineralization, Arctic Mid-Ocean Ridges
item.dc:creator: Sabina Strmić Palinkaš
Marie Wold
Rolf Birger Pedersen
Håvard Hallås Stubseid
Fredrik Sahlström
Lahaye Yann
Leif-Erik Rydland Pedersen
Jorge E. Spangenberg
item.description: The extinct Gnitahei seafloor massive sulfide (SMS) deposit was discovered in 2019 on the ultraslow spreading Mohns Ridge (Arctic Mid-Ocean Ridge System, 72.8°N, 3.81°E, 3022–2760 mbsl). It provides a unique opportunity to study subseafloor hydrothermal processes without drilling, as tectonic uplift and ongoing mass wasting expose the deeper parts of the hydrothermal system. The deposit is hosted by hydrothermally altered basalts within the footwall of a NE–SW striking normal fault, in proximity to the active Fåvne Vent Field. Driven by extensional tectonics and facilitated by the thin oceanic crust along the ultraslow-spreading Mohns Ridge, hydrothermal circulation likely extends deep into the serpentinized upper mantle. Hydrothermal alteration exhibits vertical zonation. Chloritization accompanied by remobilization of silica, LILEs, and REEs dominate the deeper parts of the system. In contrast, silicification associated with an enrichment in LILEs and REEs characterizes the upper part of the Gnitahei SMS deposit. The paleosurface is clearly marked by a layer of red jasper. The sulfide mineralization is dominated by pyrite and marcasite, with euhedral pyrite prevailing in deeper zones and colloform pyrite being more abundant near the paleosurface. Native gold occurs mostly as inclusions in pyrite. Trace-element data obtained from pyrite, including elevated Ni and Co concentrations and a high Au/Ag ratios, are consistent with fluid interaction with underlying ultramafic rocks. Sulfur isotope composition of pyrite reveals a strong 34S enrichment (bulk δ34SVCDT = 11.1–15.9‰; in-situ δ34SVCDT = 10.8–17.3‰), indicating a significant contribution of reduced seawater sulfate, which constitutes ~50% of the total sulfur according to a two-component mixing model. The high influx of reduced sulfate is attributed to reducing conditions driven by serpentinization of ultramafic lithologies. Under these conditions, seawater sulfate can be reduced prior to anhydrite deposition, allowing isotopically heavy sulfur to infiltrate the hydrothermal system. Depth-dependent decreases in δ34S values likely reflect greater contributions of reduced seawater sulfate in shallower zones and/or enhanced fractionation at lower temperatures. Published: 2026-06-29T16:00:39+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.600 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sabina Strmić Palinkaš; Marie Wold; Rolf Birger Pedersen; Håvard Hallås Stubseid; Fredrik Sahlström; Lahaye Yann; Leif-Erik Rydland Pedersen; Jorge E. Spangenberg

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123585

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

The extinct Gnitahei seafloor massive sulfide (SMS) deposit was discovered in 2019 on the ultraslow spreading Mohns Ridge (Arctic Mid-Ocean Ridge System, 72.8°N, 3.81°E, 3022–2760 mbsl). It provides a unique opportunity to study subseafloor hydrothermal processes without drilling, as tectonic uplift and ongoing mass wasting expose the deeper parts of the hydrothermal system. The deposit is hosted by hydrothermally altered basalts within the footwall of a NE–SW striking normal fault, in proximity to the active Fåvne Vent Field. Driven by extensional tectonics and facilitated by the thin oceanic crust along the ultraslow-spreading Mohns Ridge, hydrothermal circulation likely extends deep into the serpentinized upper mantle. Hydrothermal alteration exhibits vertical zonation. Chloritization accompanied by remobilization of silica, LILEs, and REEs dominate the deeper parts of the system. In contrast, silicification associated with an enrichment in LILEs and REEs characterizes the upper part of the Gnitahei SMS deposit. The paleosurface is clearly marked by a layer of red jasper. The sulfide mineralization is dominated by pyrite and marcasite, with euhedral pyrite prevailing in deeper zones and colloform pyrite being more abundant near the paleosurface. Native gold occurs mostly as inclusions in pyrite. Trace-element data obtained from pyrite, including elevated Ni and Co concentrations and a high Au/Ag ratios, are consistent with fluid interaction with underlying ultramafic rocks. Sulfur isotope composition of pyrite reveals a strong 34S enrichment (bulk δ34SVCDT = 11.1–15.9‰; in-situ δ34SVCDT = 10.8–17.3‰), indicating a significant contribution of reduced seawater sulfate, which constitutes ~50% of the total sulfur according to a two-component mixing model. The high influx of reduced sulfate is attributed to reducing conditions driven by serpentinization of ultramafic lithologies. Under these conditions, seawater sulfate can be reduced prior to anhydrite deposition, allowing isotopically heavy sulfur to infiltrate the hydrothermal system. Depth-dependent decreases in δ34S values likely reflect greater contributions of reduced seawater sulfate in shallower zones and/or enhanced fractionation at lower temperatures.

item.title: Precise age constraints for the Huangjindong gold deposit, South China
item.dc:creator: Yulong Lu
Zengxian Luo
Fei Wang
Peng Wang
Jianzhong Huang
Huan Li
Qingquan Liu
Guojun Dong
Chunhua Wen
Defeng Shi
Enbo Tu
Yang Liu
Zhen Tan
item.description: The large Huangjindong gold deposit, which was positioned in the Jiangnan Orogen, has developed through an early scheelite-gold mineralization epoch and a later wolframite mineralization epoch. The ore-forming age remains poorly constrained, ranging from 462 Ma to 129.7 Ma, mainly due to a lack of appropriate dating minerals and uncertainties in mineral paragenesis. The current work identified hydrothermal rutile paragenetic with gold-bearing arsenopyrite as well as post-mineralization wolframite. These rutile and wolframite grains were subjected to in-situ LA-ICP-MS U–Pb dating, resulting in an age of 380.6 ± 6.6 Ma for the principal ore-forming stage during the scheelite-gold epoch and 158.8 ± 6.6 Ma for the wolframite mineralization epoch. The obtained ages conform to the observed ore-forming processes, implying that the primary gold mineralization of this deposit happened in the Devonian period. Combined with recently obtained precise ages for gold deposits in South China, this work emphasizes that the Silurian–Devonian was an important period for gold mineralization. The gold mineralization event at Huangjindong is indicative of Silurian–Devonian tectono-magmatic-hydrothermal activity in South China, formed within an extensional tectonic framework associated with intracontinental orogenic evolution. This paper also highlights that in-situ dating of U-bearing accessory minerals is an effective method for precisely constraining mineralization timing in deposits characterized by multi-pulse hydrothermal activity. Published: 2026-06-29T16:05:44+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107417

Relevance: 0.596 (consider)

The large Huangjindong gold deposit, which was positioned in the Jiangnan Orogen, has developed through an early scheelite-gold mineralization epoch and a later wolframite mineralization epoch. The ore-forming age remains poorly constrained, ranging from 462 Ma to 129.7 Ma, mainly due to a lack of appropriate dating minerals and uncertainties in mineral paragenesis. These rutile and wolframite grains were subjected to in-situ LA-ICP-MS U–Pb dating, resulting in an age of 380.6 ± 6.6 Ma for the principal ore-forming stage during the scheelite-gold epoch and 158.8 ± 6.6 Ma for the wolframite mineralization epoch. The obtained ages conform to the observed ore-forming processes, implying that the primary gold mineralization of this deposit happened in the Devonian period. Combined with recently obtained precise ages for gold deposits in South China, this work emphasizes that the Silurian–Devonian was an important period for gold mineralization. The gold mineralization event at Huangjindong is indicative of Silurian–Devonian tectono-magmatic-hydrothermal activity in South China, formed within an extensional tectonic framework associated with intracontinental orogenic evolution. This paper also highlights that in-situ dating of U-bearing accessory minerals is an effective method for precisely constraining mineralization timing in deposits characterized by multi-pulse hydrothermal activity.

item.title: Rediscovery of the high-grade Viana orebody: a missing link in the Archean Cuiabá microbasin framework and the timing of a world-class BIF-hosted gold system
item.dc:creator: Daniel T. Peterle
Lydia M. Lobato
Stanislav Ulrich
Suellen O.C. Pinto
Edson R.M. Ferraz
Svetlana Tessalina
Bruno V. Ribeiro
Thales J.C. Santos
item.description: The Cuiabá world-class gold deposit, hosted by the Archean Rio das Velhas green-stone belt (Minas Gerais, Brazil), is a major gold system with >7 Moz produced. Re-cent resource delineation drilling at Levels L19 and L20 intercepted a 30-m-thick mineralized zone (Viana) at the hangingwall of the Fonte Grande Sul and Serrotinho orebodies. The Viana orebody, represents a significant (re)discovery (>15 t Au) of a historically neglected shallow target. The Viana orebody is subdivided into two domains. Domain 1 features a 15-m-thick replacement-style ore zone (15–30 g/t Au) consisting of massive sulfide at the base and a gold-bearing metachert at the top, separated from barren metachert by a mylonitic sericite-chlorite schist. Domain 2 comprises a 2-m-thick zone of carbonate-sericite-quartz schists and laminated/smoky-quartz veins (2–10 g/t Au). Multi-element geochemistry and REE + Y systematics demonstrate that the carbon-rich Viana Metachert is geochemically distinct from the main Cuiabá BIF horizon, reflecting a stronger high-temperature hydrothermal input and organic matter presence during deposition. Structurally, the Viana orebody is interpreted as the result of flexural-slip folding along its stratigraphic level during the formation of the Cuiabá F1 upright fold (D1). Domain 2 laminated veins developed along refracted cleavages, channelizing fluids into Domain 1. A duplex system formed within Domain 1 due to flexural-slip, trapping syn-deformational fluids below the thrust plane. The current cigar-shaped oreshoot geometry results from refolding during the D2 penetrative event. Geochronological data expand the temporal window of gold mineralization in the Rio das Velhas belt. Hydrothermal monazite U-Pb dating from massive sulfides (51.7 g/t Au) yielded an age of 2775 ± 23 Ma, while Re-Os ages from arsenopyrites yielded 2723 ± 25 Ma. These results identify a previously unrecognized chemical sedimentary horizon and indicate that gold mineralization occurred through multiple pulses associated with the Rio das Velhas II and Mamona tectono-magmatic events. This study requires a reinterpretation of the local lithostratigraphic column and highlights significant new exploration opportunities within the Cuiabá system. Published: 2026-06-28T14:18:32+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.590 (consider)

Relevance: 0.590 (consider)

The Cuiabá world-class gold deposit, hosted by the Archean Rio das Velhas green-stone belt (Minas Gerais, Brazil), is a major gold system with >7 Moz produced. Re-cent resource delineation drilling at Levels L19 and L20 intercepted a 30-m-thick mineralized zone (Viana) at the hangingwall of the Fonte Grande Sul and Serrotinho orebodies. The Viana orebody, represents a significant (re)discovery (>15 t Au) of a historically neglected shallow target. The Viana orebody is subdivided into two domains. Domain 1 features a 15-m-thick replacement-style ore zone (15–30 g/t Au) consisting of massive sulfide at the base and a gold-bearing metachert at the top, separated from barren metachert by a mylonitic sericite-chlorite schist. Domain 2 comprises a 2-m-thick zone of carbonate-sericite-quartz schists and laminated/smoky-quartz veins (2–10 g/t Au). Multi-element geochemistry and REE + Y systematics demonstrate that the carbon-rich Viana Metachert is geochemically distinct from the main Cuiabá BIF horizon, reflecting a stronger high-temperature hydrothermal input and organic matter presence during deposition. Structurally, the Viana orebody is interpreted as the result of flexural-slip folding along its stratigraphic level during the formation of the Cuiabá F1 upright fold (D1). Domain 2 laminated veins developed along refracted cleavages, channelizing fluids into Domain 1. A duplex system formed within Domain 1 due to flexural-slip, trapping syn-deformational fluids below the thrust plane. The current cigar-shaped oreshoot geometry results from refolding during the D2 penetrative event. Geochronological data expand the temporal window of gold mineralization in the Rio das Velhas belt. These results identify a previously unrecognized chemical sedimentary horizon and indicate that gold mineralization occurred through multiple pulses associated with the Rio das Velhas II and Mamona tectono-magmatic events. This study requires a reinterpretation of the local lithostratigraphic column and highlights significant new exploration opportunities within the Cuiabá system.

item.title: Correlations between multi-stage tectonic evolution and metallogenic processes in the proto- and paleo-tethyan domains in the sanjiang composite orogenic system, SW China
item.dc:creator: Baodi Wang
Dongbing Wang
Guochuang Yan
Xi Xu
Yunpeng Yu
Qianli Ma
Yanyun Sun
Li Cheng
Fang Zhou
Song Han
item.description: The Sanjiang Tethyan orogenic belt, a globally representative composite multi-island arc-basin system, has been a focus of ongoing debate regarding the coupling relationship between the Proto- and Paleo-Tethyan tectonic evolution and metallogenic processes. This study systematically synthesizes regional geological, geochronological, and metallogenic data to characterize the distinct tectonic units within the Sanjiang Composite Orogenic System. A tectono-metallogenic coupling model is established by integrating the spatiotemporal distribution of ophiolites, high-pressure metamorphic rocks, and representative ore deposits. The Changning-Menglian suture zone preserves a complete record of the oceanic-basin evolution from subduction to final closure, including Early Paleozoic Lancang Group accretionary complexes, Ordovician-Silurian ophiolites, Early Paleozoic high-Mg adakites, Devonian-Carboniferous oceanic island basalts, and Middle-Late Triassic eclogites. Our results demonstrate a strong genetic correlation between regional metallogenesis and tectonic settings: extensional settings control Sedex-type Pb-Zn deposits, subduction systems dominate VMS-type Cu-Pb-Zn mineralization, collisional stages develop porphyry-skarn Cu-Mo deposits, while post-collisional extension triggers Sn-W and epithermal Pb-Zn mineralization. Innovatively, this study proposes a four-stage tectonic-metallogenic evolution model for the Proto- and Paleo-Tethyan Ocean: (1) Early Paleozoic passive margin extension (Sedex-type Pb-Zn mineralization); (2) Middle Ordovician to Silurian subduction (VMS/BIF-type mineralization); (3) Late Paleozoic development of a multi-island arc-basin system (large-scale VMS-type mineralization); and (4) Middle-Late Triassic collisional orogeny (eclogite deep subduction, porphyry-skarn Cu-Mo, and Sn-W mineralization). These findings provide new insights into the multi-stage dynamic processes of Proto- and Paleo-Tethyan evolution and clarify the controlling mechanisms of diverse tectonic settings (rift → subduction → collision → intracontinental extension) on metallogenic systems. Published: 2026-06-25T23:49:32+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.588 (consider)

item.title: Ion microprobe analysis of fluorine, water, and oxygen isotopes in garnet from skarn deposits in the Tongling ore district, eastern China: Implications for coupled F-OH substitution mechanism and polymetallic mineralization
item.dc:creator: Hui Luo
Yuyao Chen
Wei Xiao
Xiaoyong Yang
Bin Fu
item.description: Skarn deposits, as integral components of magmatic-hydrothermal systems, represent major global resources of economically critical metals. Volatile constituents such as fluorine (F), chlorine (Cl), and sulfur (S) play fundamental roles in regulating metal complexation, transport efficiency, and precipitation within hydrothermal fluids. Despite this recognized importance, the partitioning behavior of F among melt, minerals, and fluid phases and its influence on metal selectivity across different skarn deposits remain insufficiently constrained. This study investigates representative skarn deposits from the world-class Tongling ore district in eastern China to evaluate the substitution mechanisms and partitioning behavior of F and hydroxyl (OH) in garnet, together with oxygen isotope systematics, using high-precision ion microprobe analysis. The objective is to clarify the metallogenic role of the volatiles in polymetallic skarn mineralization. The principal findings are as follows: (1) F and hydroxyl incorporation in garnet are dominantly coupled, reflecting defect-related substitution mechanisms; (2) a first-order quantitative estimate of the garnet-fluid F partition coefficient (DFGrt−Fluid = ∼0.074) indicates that F is highly mobile in hydrothermal fluids but exhibits limited structural incorporation in garnet; (3) elevated F contents in garnet from skarn Cu-Mo deposits imply the development of hydroxyl-fluoride complexes in ore-forming fluids, potentially enhancing Mo transport and precipitation; and (4) calculated δ18Ofluid values decrease from early skarn stages (6.3‰−11.2‰, Vienna standard mean ocean water) to late quartz-sulfide stages (3.1‰−7.6‰), indicating dominantly magmatic fluid sources with minor input of ancient meteoric water during the later stages of mineralization. Collectively, integrated secondary ion mass spectrometry determinations of F and H2O contents, combined with garnet oxygen isotopes, provide quantitative constraints on F partitioning behavior in skarn systems and elucidate the fundamental role of volatiles in controlling metal transport and enrichment during polymetallic mineralization. Published: 2026-06-25T20:49:54+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.582 (consider)

Skarn deposits, as integral components of magmatic-hydrothermal systems, represent major global resources of economically critical metals. Volatile constituents such as fluorine (F), chlorine (Cl), and sulfur (S) play fundamental roles in regulating metal complexation, transport efficiency, and precipitation within hydrothermal fluids. item.title: Si–O isotopes and trace elements of detrital zircons record magmatic sources and environments of the early continental crust
item.dc:creator: Kai Lei
Hao Wang
Peng Liou
Jilian Jiang
Jinghui Guo
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: The formation and evolution of the Archaean continental crust record the history of the early Earth, although its magmatic sources and magmatic environments remain controversial. Detrital zircons may preserve geochemical signatures of both extinct and surviving crustal rocks, providing insights into crustal formation. We present in situ Si–O isotope and trace element data for detrital zircons from Caozhuang (3.84–3.53 Ga) and Kaapvaal (3.55–3.45 Ga and 3.28–3.11 Ga). The zircons have δ30Si values of −0.50‰ to −0.12‰, corresponding to modeled melt δ30Si values that are higher than those of the Igneous Array. The 3.84–3.53 Ga Caozhuang and 3.26–3.11 Ga Kaapvaal zircons display elevated δ18O values (6.03–7.25‰) compared to mantle zircons, whereas those from 3.55 to 3.45 Ga and one 3.28 Ga Kaapvaal zircons display “mantle-like” δ18O signatures (5.49–6.05‰). Consistently high δ30Si values in the zircons indicate a contribution from supracrustal silicified materials. This suggests that the “mantle-like” δ18O values may not necessarily reflect a mantle source. Instead, these δ18O signatures may result from metasomatism of the source by low-δ18O fluids derived from komatiites, or may reflect the limited sensitivity of O isotopes to trace supracrustal materials in ancient zircons. By combing the Si–O isotopes of zircons with their Tht/Nb (16–157) and Ut/Nb (50–417) ratios, Dy/Yb ratios (0.2–0.4), we suggest that the parent melt of zircons have geochemical affinities to arc magmas. Compared to Jack Hills zircons, the absence of pelitic material and meteoric water signatures suggests that these zircons were formed far from subaerial continents. Published: 2026-06-23T07:44:55+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.582 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kai Lei; Hao Wang; Peng Liou; Jilian Jiang; Jinghui Guo; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108193

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

The formation and evolution of the Archaean continental crust record the history of the early Earth, although its magmatic sources and magmatic environments remain controversial. Detrital zircons may preserve geochemical signatures of both extinct and surviving crustal rocks, providing insights into crustal formation. We present in situ Si–O isotope and trace element data for detrital zircons from Caozhuang (3.84–3.53 Ga) and Kaapvaal (3.55–3.45 Ga and 3.28–3.11 Ga). The zircons have δ30Si values of −0.50‰ to −0.12‰, corresponding to modeled melt δ30Si values that are higher than those of the Igneous Array. The 3.84–3.53 Ga Caozhuang and 3.26–3.11 Ga Kaapvaal zircons display elevated δ18O values (6.03–7.25‰) compared to mantle zircons, whereas those from 3.55 to 3.45 Ga and one 3.28 Ga Kaapvaal zircons display “mantle-like” δ18O signatures (5.49–6.05‰). Consistently high δ30Si values in the zircons indicate a contribution from supracrustal silicified materials. This suggests that the “mantle-like” δ18O values may not necessarily reflect a mantle source. Instead, these δ18O signatures may result from metasomatism of the source by low-δ18O fluids derived from komatiites, or may reflect the limited sensitivity of O isotopes to trace supracrustal materials in ancient zircons. By combing the Si–O isotopes of zircons with their Tht/Nb (16–157) and Ut/Nb (50–417) ratios, Dy/Yb ratios (0.2–0.4), we suggest that the parent melt of zircons have geochemical affinities to arc magmas. Compared to Jack Hills zircons, the absence of pelitic material and meteoric water signatures suggests that these zircons were formed far from subaerial continents.

item.title: Mid-Cretaceous porphyritic magmatism in the Beartooth Mountains of the northern Laramide foreland and its connection to Frontier Formation sediment dispersal in Bighorn basin, Montana-Wyoming (USA), and Cordilleran tectonism
item.dc:creator: Jacob O. Thacker
Nicholas A. Brailer
Gary S. Michelfelder
Barry J. Shaulis
Rebecca A. VanderLeest
Snir Attia
Eddy Cunningham
item.description: Porphyritic intrusions in the southeast Beartooth Mountains and porphyritic cobbles from the Torchlight conglomerate of the Cenomanian Frontier Formation in Bighorn basin (Montana-Wyoming, USA) bear striking resemblance. We utilize geologic mapping, petrography, geochemistry, and geochronology to characterize rocks from both locations and test whether the Beartooth Mountains area was the provenance for Bighorn basin cobbles. Mapping on Line Creek Plateau in the Beartooth Mountains shows three units with porphyritic phenocrysts consisting dominantly of plagioclase, plagioclase + potassium feldspar, and plagioclase + quartz. Petrography shows embayed quartz, opacitic rims, and zoned plagioclase. Geochemistry characterizes the intrusions as trachydacite to trachyte and trace elements exhibit enrichment in light rare earth elements and slight depletion in heavy rare earth elements relative to primitive mantle. Zircon U-Pb dating shows substantial Archean inheritance and ca. 104−92 Ma dates; some samples display multiple mid-Cretaceous date populations. Bighorn basin porphyritic cobbles exhibit similar modal mineralogy, near-identical geochemistry, and Archean inheritance and mid-Cretaceous geochronology. We suggest the porphyritic cobbles were sourced from basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions now exposed in the Beartooth Mountains, given that geochemistry does not agree with coeval volcanic sources along the Frontier Formation depositional fairway (Idaho batholith) or sedimentary-hosted Beartooth Mountains porphyritic sills intruded into Cambrian shale. Stratigraphic evidence does not support kilometer-scale mid-Cretaceous Beartooth Mountains exhumation to expose basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions, and we therefore present a volcanic hypothesis to bring rocks to the surface. This mid-Cretaceous multimillion-year magmatism is unexplained by tectonic models for the north-central Laramide foreland, and thus we further explore the emplacement processes, spatiotemporal significance, and tectonic implications of these rocks. Published: 2026-06-24T20:49:18+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.578 (consider)

Porphyritic intrusions in the southeast Beartooth Mountains and porphyritic cobbles from the Torchlight conglomerate of the Cenomanian Frontier Formation in Bighorn basin (Montana-Wyoming, USA) bear striking resemblance. We utilize geologic mapping, petrography, geochemistry, and geochronology to characterize rocks from both locations and test whether the Beartooth Mountains area was the provenance for Bighorn basin cobbles. Mapping on Line Creek Plateau in the Beartooth Mountains shows three units with porphyritic phenocrysts consisting dominantly of plagioclase, plagioclase + potassium feldspar, and plagioclase + quartz. Petrography shows embayed quartz, opacitic rims, and zoned plagioclase. Geochemistry characterizes the intrusions as trachydacite to trachyte and trace elements exhibit enrichment in light rare earth elements and slight depletion in heavy rare earth elements relative to primitive mantle. Zircon U-Pb dating shows substantial Archean inheritance and ca. 104−92 Ma dates; some samples display multiple mid-Cretaceous date populations. Bighorn basin porphyritic cobbles exhibit similar modal mineralogy, near-identical geochemistry, and Archean inheritance and mid-Cretaceous geochronology. We suggest the porphyritic cobbles were sourced from basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions now exposed in the Beartooth Mountains, given that geochemistry does not agree with coeval volcanic sources along the Frontier Formation depositional fairway (Idaho batholith) or sedimentary-hosted Beartooth Mountains porphyritic sills intruded into Cambrian shale. Stratigraphic evidence does not support kilometer-scale mid-Cretaceous Beartooth Mountains exhumation to expose basement-hosted porphyritic intrusions, and we therefore present a volcanic hypothesis to bring rocks to the surface. This mid-Cretaceous multimillion-year magmatism is unexplained by tectonic models for the north-central Laramide foreland, and thus we further explore the emplacement processes, spatiotemporal significance, and tectonic implications of these rocks.

item.title: vDetrital zircon reveals secular evolution of granite petrogenesis from early Earth geodynamics to supercontinent cycles
item.dc:creator: Mingde Lang
Zhaochong Zhang
Tom E. Sheldrake
Ruixuan Zhang
Zhiguo Cheng
Yining Shi
item.description: The secular evolution of granitoid magmatism provides a key to understanding the transition from early Earth’s geodynamic regimes to modern plate tectonics and the co-evolution of continental crust with supercontinent cycles. Detrital zircons preserve a continuous record of this evolution, but previous provenance classifications have been limited to a binary I-S-type scheme, overlooking A-type, M−type, and non-granitic sources. Here we present a two-step machine-learning classifier (Random Forest, 93.2 % overall accuracy), trained on 8,101 magmatic zircons with known host-rock context, that discriminates among I-, S-, A-, M−type granitoid and non-granitic sources based on 18 trace-element features. Applied to 11,591 detrital zircons older than 500 Ma, the classifier reveals first-order Precambrian trends in granite petrogenesis and their links to supercontinent evolution. S-type zircons appear as early as ∼ 4.3 Ga, supporting Hadean liquid water and supracrustal recycling. M−type zircons are first recognized after ∼ 3.5 Ga and increase markedly beginning just before ∼ 2.0 Ga, suggesting a progressive involvement of mantle-derived magmas consistent with a transition to more widespread horizontal tectonics. I-type zircon abundances display declines coincident with the Great Oxidation Event and Neoproterozoic Oxidation Event, motivating a testable hypothesis that links deep reducing-gas fluxes, atmospheric O2 sinks, and redox conditions unfavorable for typical oxidized I-type granitoid magmatism. A-type zircon enrichment intervals, especially around ∼ 2.7–2.5 Ga, ∼1.85–1.75 Ga, and ∼ 1.25–1.10 Ga, broadly correspond to lithospheric thinning and post-orogenic to intraplate extension, suggesting that A-type zircon abundance records lithospheric thermal reorganization, pointing to gravitational delamination as a dominant control. By explicitly including all granitoid types and non-granitic sources and framing the results within the context of supercontinent cycles, our approach provides a more complete and quantitative framework for reading Earth’s deep-time history from detrital zircon archives, offering new insights into the coupled evolution of the continental lithosphere, surface environments, and global geodynamics. Published: 2026-06-27T23:04:54+00:00 Venue: Gondwana Research Score: 0.577 (consider)

The secular evolution of granitoid magmatism provides a key to understanding the transition from early Earth’s geodynamic regimes to modern plate tectonics and the co-evolution of continental crust with supercontinent cycles. Detrital zircons preserve a continuous record of this evolution, but previous provenance classifications have been limited to a binary I-S-type scheme, overlooking A-type, M−type, and non-granitic sources. Here we present a two-step machine-learning classifier (Random Forest, 93.2 % overall accuracy), trained on 8,101 magmatic zircons with known host-rock context, that discriminates among I-, S-, A-, M−type granitoid and non-granitic sources based on 18 trace-element features. Applied to 11,591 detrital zircons older than 500 Ma, the classifier reveals first-order Precambrian trends in granite petrogenesis and their links to supercontinent evolution. M−type zircons are first recognized after ∼ 3.5 Ga and increase markedly beginning just before ∼ 2.0 Ga, suggesting a progressive involvement of mantle-derived magmas consistent with a transition to more widespread horizontal tectonics. I-type zircon abundances display declines coincident with the Great Oxidation Event and Neoproterozoic Oxidation Event, motivating a testable hypothesis that links deep reducing-gas fluxes, atmospheric O2 sinks, and redox conditions unfavorable for typical oxidized I-type granitoid magmatism. A-type zircon enrichment intervals, especially around ∼ 2.7–2.5 Ga, ∼1.85–1.75 Ga, and ∼ 1.25–1.10 Ga, broadly correspond to lithospheric thinning and post-orogenic to intraplate extension, suggesting that A-type zircon abundance records lithospheric thermal reorganization, pointing to gravitational delamination as a dominant control. By explicitly including all granitoid types and non-granitic sources and framing the results within the context of supercontinent cycles, our approach provides a more complete and quantitative framework for reading Earth’s deep-time history from detrital zircon archives, offering new insights into the coupled evolution of the continental lithosphere, surface environments, and global geodynamics.

item.title: Mineralogical and geochemical insights into the Duxiushan granite-pegmatite system in Anqing area, Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic belt, eastern China: Implications for critical metal mineralization
item.dc:creator: Chongyang Yuan
Xiaoyong Yang
Zhuang Zhao
Xuanyang Feng
Lingkang Ma
Beicai Zhang
Caihong Gan
item.description: The concealed Duxiushan intrusion, located in the Anqing district of the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt in eastern China, is a newly recognized granite–pegmatite complex that offers critical insights into the late-stage evolution of highly fractionated granitic magmatism in this region. To investigate its petrogenesis and potential for critical metal enrichment, we employed a combination of monazite and apatite U–Pb dating (which circumvents the common problem of zircon metamictization in highly evolved systems), whole-rock geochemistry, Sr–Nd isotopes, and mineral chemistry. The obtained ages, ranging from approximately 130 to 124 Ma, indicate emplacement during Early Cretaceous lithospheric extension, linked to the rollback of the Paleo-Pacific Plate. The two-mica granite, muscovite granite, and pegmatite exhibit consistent mineralogical and geochemical trends, suggesting that they originated from a common crustal melt primarily derived from the partial melting of ancient metasedimentary rocks, as indicated by their enriched Sr-Nd isotopic compositions, with minimal mantle-derived contributions. Systematic enrichment in SiO2, Rb, and F, alongside depletion in Ba, Sr, and Eu, reflects significant fractional crystallization. Compositional analysis of mica and apatite reveals a decrease in oxygen fugacity and the evolution of F-dominated magmatic fluids under reduced conditions, creating favorable conditions for the retention of incompatible elements during magma evolution. LA–ICP–MS mapping identifies Rb concentrations exceeding 1500 ppm in pegmatitic muscovite, confirming significant mineral-scale Rb enrichment. These findings demonstrate that the concealed Duxiushan granite–pegmatite system represents a late-stage product of crustal anatexis during Early Cretaceous extension in the Middle-Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt. Published: 2026-06-29T23:11:49+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chongyang Yuan; Xiaoyong Yang; Zhuang Zhao; Xuanyang Feng; Lingkang Ma; Beicai Zhang; Caihong Gan

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107199

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

The concealed Duxiushan intrusion, located in the Anqing district of the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt in eastern China, is a newly recognized granite–pegmatite complex that offers critical insights into the late-stage evolution of highly fractionated granitic magmatism in this region. To investigate its petrogenesis and potential for critical metal enrichment, we employed a combination of monazite and apatite U–Pb dating (which circumvents the common problem of zircon metamictization in highly evolved systems), whole-rock geochemistry, Sr–Nd isotopes, and mineral chemistry. The obtained ages, ranging from approximately 130 to 124 Ma, indicate emplacement during Early Cretaceous lithospheric extension, linked to the rollback of the Paleo-Pacific Plate. The two-mica granite, muscovite granite, and pegmatite exhibit consistent mineralogical and geochemical trends, suggesting that they originated from a common crustal melt primarily derived from the partial melting of ancient metasedimentary rocks, as indicated by their enriched Sr-Nd isotopic compositions, with minimal mantle-derived contributions. Systematic enrichment in SiO2, Rb, and F, alongside depletion in Ba, Sr, and Eu, reflects significant fractional crystallization. Compositional analysis of mica and apatite reveals a decrease in oxygen fugacity and the evolution of F-dominated magmatic fluids under reduced conditions, creating favorable conditions for the retention of incompatible elements during magma evolution. LA–ICP–MS mapping identifies Rb concentrations exceeding 1500 ppm in pegmatitic muscovite, confirming significant mineral-scale Rb enrichment. These findings demonstrate that the concealed Duxiushan granite–pegmatite system represents a late-stage product of crustal anatexis during Early Cretaceous extension in the Middle-Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt.

item.title: Lower crustal modification at the margin of the North China Craton: Multi-proxy geochemical and isotopic imaging constraints from the Late Mesozoic granitoids in Jiaodong Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Can-Can Wang
Li-Qiang Yang
Xue Gao
Wen-Yan He
Lei Ju
Dong Xie
Long Wang
Shi-Yao Zhai
Wei Yang
Nan Li
Zheng-Jiang Ding
Jun Deng
item.description: The evolution of continental crust is a fundamental issue in solid Earth science. Granitoids, as key products of the lower crust, record these processes. The Jiaodong Peninsula hosts three stages of Late Mesozoic granitoids and provides insights into cratonic lower crustal modification. This study integrates geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Hf-O isotopes to constrain these processes. Nb-Ta-Zr-Hf elemental and Hf-O isotopic compositions define a NW-SE trending geochemical boundary (∼121°E). This boundary separates the strongly modified Jiaobei Terrane from the weakly modified Sulu Terrane and aligns with tectonic subdivisions along the Wulian-Yantai fault zone. Three stages of lower crustal modification are identified. Stage I granitoids (166–141 Ma) show variable εHf(t) values (−26.5 to −13.1), δ18O values (4.58–8.93‰), and low Mg# (16–34). These features indicate partial melting of a thickened ancient lower crust during subduction of the Paleo-Pacific Plate. Stage II granitoids (133–121 Ma) display higher εHf(t) values (–23.0 to −11.2), elevated δ18O values (8.09–9.03‰), and Mg# of 26–58. These characteristics indicate melting of a weakly modified lower crust triggered by underplating of enriched mantle materials metasomatized by high-δ18O fluids during slab rollback. Stage III granitoids (120–107 Ma) exhibit high εHf(t) values (–22.3 to −13.9), lower δ18O values (5.34–8.09‰), and higher Mg# (17–64). These data indicate melting of a strongly modified lower crust influenced by enriched mantle materials metasomatized by low-δ18O melts and sediments during continued slab rollback. Integrated petrogenetic, spatiotemporal, and geophysical evidence supports a progressive lower crustal modification model controlled by subduction and slab rollback processes. Published: 2026-06-26T16:44:32+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.572 (consider)

The evolution of continental crust is a fundamental issue in solid Earth science. Granitoids, as key products of the lower crust, record these processes. The Jiaodong Peninsula hosts three stages of Late Mesozoic granitoids and provides insights into cratonic lower crustal modification. This study integrates geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Hf-O isotopes to constrain these processes. Three stages of lower crustal modification are identified. Stage I granitoids (166–141 Ma) show variable εHf(t) values (−26.5 to −13.1), δ18O values (4.58–8.93‰), and low Mg# (16–34). Stage II granitoids (133–121 Ma) display higher εHf(t) values (–23.0 to −11.2), elevated δ18O values (8.09–9.03‰), and Mg# of 26–58. These characteristics indicate melting of a weakly modified lower crust triggered by underplating of enriched mantle materials metasomatized by high-δ18O fluids during slab rollback. Stage III granitoids (120–107 Ma) exhibit high εHf(t) values (–22.3 to −13.9), lower δ18O values (5.34–8.09‰), and higher Mg# (17–64). These data indicate melting of a strongly modified lower crust influenced by enriched mantle materials metasomatized by low-δ18O melts and sediments during continued slab rollback. Integrated petrogenetic, spatiotemporal, and geophysical evidence supports a progressive lower crustal modification model controlled by subduction and slab rollback processes.

item.title: Genetic types, mineralization patterns and exploration implications of ore-hosting fractures in porphyry-type deposits
item.dc:creator: Mao-Chun Zhao
Yan-Jun Wang
Hai-Jun Yu
Xi-Lian Chen
Da-Zhao Wang
Jin-Song Yang
Yan Zhang
Cheng-Biao Leng
item.description: Fractures concentrating around shallow granitic plutons are the key deposition site for porphyry-type ores. Early studies have proposed various formation mechanisms for different types of fractures, but these mechanisms are yet to be well integrated with the porphyry ore-forming processes. Here, we present a synthetic study on the formation mechanism and influence factors of fracture network development in/around the ore-causative porphyries during their emplacement, cooling/solidification, and alteration. According to their order of appearance, the fractures in porphyry systems can be divided into three types: (1) intrusion-induced type, (2) magma cooling-related type (i.e., cooling fractures), and (3) magmatic fluid-related hydraulic type (i.e., hydraulic fractures). Intrusion-induced fractures are formed by the compression on wallrocks exerted by magma emplacement, and are mostly distributed in the wallrocks around the pluton apex. Cooling fractures are only developed within the porphyries during magma cooling and solidification. Hydraulic fractures are largely formed by magmatic-hydrothermal pressure-driven cracking. The occurrence, geometry, orientation, and ore grade of the porphyry-type orebodies are all controlled by these three fracture systems, which offers insights for ore prospecting: (1) Intrusion-induced fracture-controlled orebodies are bell-shaped overlying the porphyry bodies, with ore grade gradually dropping away from the porphyries. The veins are relatively straight, and generally comprise a steep- (major) and a shallow- (minor) dipping group. They are few-mm to few-cm wide, and several meters long; (2) Cooling fracture-controlled orebodies are also bell-shaped and commonly developed in the densely-fractured porphyry apex, with the ore grade decreasing away from the porphyry margin toward depth. The veins are generally few-tens of cm long and within a few-mm wide, and are homogeneously distributed with no preferred orientation; (3) Hydraulic fracture-controlled orebodies are generally developed as pipes in the axial part of the porphyries, and can vertically extend over 1000 m. The veins are mostly steep and few-mm to few-cm wide, and several to tens of meters long. Published: 2026-06-24T16:19:40+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.569 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mao-Chun Zhao; Yan-Jun Wang; Hai-Jun Yu; Xi-Lian Chen; Da-Zhao Wang; Jin-Song Yang; Yan Zhang; Cheng-Biao Leng

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107412

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

Fractures concentrating around shallow granitic plutons are the key deposition site for porphyry-type ores. Early studies have proposed various formation mechanisms for different types of fractures, but these mechanisms are yet to be well integrated with the porphyry ore-forming processes. Here, we present a synthetic study on the formation mechanism and influence factors of fracture network development in/around the ore-causative porphyries during their emplacement, cooling/solidification, and alteration. According to their order of appearance, the fractures in porphyry systems can be divided into three types: (1) intrusion-induced type, (2) magma cooling-related type (i.e., cooling fractures), and (3) magmatic fluid-related hydraulic type (i.e., hydraulic fractures). Intrusion-induced fractures are formed by the compression on wallrocks exerted by magma emplacement, and are mostly distributed in the wallrocks around the pluton apex. Cooling fractures are only developed within the porphyries during magma cooling and solidification. Hydraulic fractures are largely formed by magmatic-hydrothermal pressure-driven cracking. The occurrence, geometry, orientation, and ore grade of the porphyry-type orebodies are all controlled by these three fracture systems, which offers insights for ore prospecting: (1) Intrusion-induced fracture-controlled orebodies are bell-shaped overlying the porphyry bodies, with ore grade gradually dropping away from the porphyries. The veins are relatively straight, and generally comprise a steep- (major) and a shallow- (minor) dipping group. They are few-mm to few-cm wide, and several meters long; (2) Cooling fracture-controlled orebodies are also bell-shaped and commonly developed in the densely-fractured porphyry apex, with the ore grade decreasing away from the porphyry margin toward depth. The veins are generally few-tens of cm long and within a few-mm wide, and are homogeneously distributed with no preferred orientation; (3) Hydraulic fracture-controlled orebodies are generally developed as pipes in the axial part of the porphyries, and can vertically extend over 1000 m. The veins are mostly steep and few-mm to few-cm wide, and several to tens of meters long.

item.title: Mid-Miocene high-temperature magmatism, a case study of the Sakya dome: Implications for the removal of the Himalayan orogenic root
item.dc:creator: Hongli Wang
Jinjiang Zhang
Yunsong Fan
Jianlei Zheng
Yihan Wang
item.description: Intense crustal anatexis, metamorphism, and structural deformation occurred throughout the collisional orogeny, preserving crucial records and constraints on the thermal and tectonic evolution. During the mid-Miocene, the Himalayan magmatism, including the leucogranites, adakite-like, and ultrapotassic rocks, culminated, coeval with (ultra)high-temperature metamorphism. However, the heat source and the deep process in the mid-Miocene Himalaya remain debated. This study presents results of U-(Th)-Pb dating of monazite and zircon, whole-rock geochemical and isotopic analyses, and three independent thermometric estimates for porphyritic granites and granite porphyries from the Sakya dome, northern Himalaya. The porphyritic granites and granite porphyries yield ages of 13.9−13.0 Ma and 12.6−11.6 Ma, respectively. Geochemical and isotopic analyses indicate the former were derived from the Greater Himalayan Crystalline (GHC), while the latter likely originated from a mixed source comprising ∼80% GHC and ∼20% Indian thickened lower crust materials. Estimated results from three thermometers consistently indicate elevated temperatures during both anatexis and subsequent magma evolution, implying an abnormally high-temperature background. Integrating evidence from the mid-Miocene Himalayan magmatism, (ultra)high-temperature metamorphism and regional tectonics, this paper adopts a model of the delamination of the Indian lithospheric mantle, which triggered the asthenospheric upwelling and additional heat input, thereby driving the (ultra)high-temperature magmatism and metamorphism, extensive anatexis at multiple depths, and diverse magmatism involving contributions from the GHC, Lesser Himalayan Sequence, Indian thickened lower crust, and the mantle in the mid-Miocene Himalaya. This delamination may signify the removal of the Himalayan orogenic root in the mid-Miocene, after which orogenesis persisted under continued convergence between the Indian and Asian plates. Published: 2026-06-29T19:34:22+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.565 (consider)

This delamination may signify the removal of the Himalayan orogenic root in the mid-Miocene, after which orogenesis persisted under continued convergence between the Indian and Asian plates.

item.title: Progressive mantle melting and crust–mantle–fluid hybridization in fluorite-bearing Sn–Nb ± Mo-rich alkaline granites of the Jos Plateau, central Nigeria
item.dc:creator: Vandi Dlama Kamaunji
Hafizullah Abba Ahmed
Lian-Xun Wang
Ioan V. Sanislav
Musa Bala Girei
Yu-Xiang Zhu
Xin-xin Liang
Xin Chen
Mabrouk Sami
item.description: Alkaline granites of the Jos Plateau Tin Belt (central Nigeria) host Sn-Nb-Mo mineralization. However, their petrogenesis, metal enrichment, and tectono-geodynamic evolution remain poorly constrained. This study integrates whole-rock geochemistry and Sr-Nd isotopes, amphibole-fluorite chemistry, and zircon U-Pb geochronology from the Buji, Jos-Bukuru (JBK), and Kigom (KGM) complexes. Zircon U-Pb ages reveal a north-to-south younging trend from Buji (∼179 Ma) through JBK (∼168 Ma) to KGM (∼166 Ma). Buji barren granites exhibit the most negative εNd(t) (–4.4 to –4.2), least radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7065–0.7066), and highest Neodymium Crustal Index (NCI = 0.31), indicating an enriched mantle source with strong crustal input. JBK Sn–Nb biotite-amphibole granites show intermediate εNd(t) (–3.3 to –3.0), NCI (0.25), and moderately radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7066–0.7173), suggesting an enriched mantle source with moderate crustal input and selective hydrothermal overprint. KGM Mo–Nb arfvedsonite–riebeckite granites exhibit near-chondritic εNd(t) (–1.1 to –0.4), lowest NCI (0.25), and most radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i)r (0.7139–0.7206), reflecting extreme fluid-mediated metasomatism on a mantle-dominated magma. Decoupled fO2 distinguishes oxidized JBK Ca-amphiboles from reduced KGM Na-amphiboles hosting oxidized zircons. Mass balance modeling indicates ∼40–50 vol% feldspar-dominated fractional crystallization coupled with ∼30–40 vol% upper crustal assimilation. JBK granites show Sn-Nb enrichment (4–126 ppm; 37–910 ppm), while KGM granites exhibit high Nb with extreme Zn-Pb-Cu (≤1326, 1054, 845 ppm), consistent with Mo-enriched systems. Fluorite compositions (F = 52–56 wt%) record F-rich reducing fluids that promoted HFSE-REE-Sn-Nb-Mo mobility. A four-stage model explains Sn-Nb-Mo enrichment in JBK and KGM: (I) magmatic enrichment; (II) magmatic-hydrothermal transition; (III) hydrothermal veining and stockworks formation; (IV) supergene placer formation. Transtension triggered adiabatic melting of metasomatized mantle, generating alkaline magmas that ascended along shear zones to form shallowly emplaced (2.6–8.7 km) anorogenic alkaline complexes. Fertile global analogues share this convergence of transtensional shearing, crustal assimilation, and F-rich fluid flux. Published: 2026-06-25T15:14:33+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.562 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vandi Dlama Kamaunji; Hafizullah Abba Ahmed; Lian-Xun Wang; Ioan V. Sanislav; Musa Bala Girei; Yu-Xiang Zhu; Xin-xin Liang; Xin Chen; Mabrouk Sami

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107405

Relevance: 0.562 (consider)

Alkaline granites of the Jos Plateau Tin Belt (central Nigeria) host Sn-Nb-Mo mineralization. However, their petrogenesis, metal enrichment, and tectono-geodynamic evolution remain poorly constrained. This study integrates whole-rock geochemistry and Sr-Nd isotopes, amphibole-fluorite chemistry, and zircon U-Pb geochronology from the Buji, Jos-Bukuru (JBK), and Kigom (KGM) complexes. Zircon U-Pb ages reveal a north-to-south younging trend from Buji (∼179 Ma) through JBK (∼168 Ma) to KGM (∼166 Ma). Buji barren granites exhibit the most negative εNd(t) (–4.4 to –4.2), least radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7065–0.7066), and highest Neodymium Crustal Index (NCI = 0.31), indicating an enriched mantle source with strong crustal input. JBK Sn–Nb biotite-amphibole granites show intermediate εNd(t) (–3.3 to –3.0), NCI (0.25), and moderately radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i) (0.7066–0.7173), suggesting an enriched mantle source with moderate crustal input and selective hydrothermal overprint. KGM Mo–Nb arfvedsonite–riebeckite granites exhibit near-chondritic εNd(t) (–1.1 to –0.4), lowest NCI (0.25), and most radiogenic 87Sr/86Sr(i)r (0.7139–0.7206), reflecting extreme fluid-mediated metasomatism on a mantle-dominated magma. Decoupled fO2 distinguishes oxidized JBK Ca-amphiboles from reduced KGM Na-amphiboles hosting oxidized zircons. Mass balance modeling indicates ∼40–50 vol% feldspar-dominated fractional crystallization coupled with ∼30–40 vol% upper crustal assimilation. JBK granites show Sn-Nb enrichment (4–126 ppm; 37–910 ppm), while KGM granites exhibit high Nb with extreme Zn-Pb-Cu (≤1326, 1054, 845 ppm), consistent with Mo-enriched systems. Fluorite compositions (F = 52–56 wt%) record F-rich reducing fluids that promoted HFSE-REE-Sn-Nb-Mo mobility. A four-stage model explains Sn-Nb-Mo enrichment in JBK and KGM: (I) magmatic enrichment; (II) magmatic-hydrothermal transition; (III) hydrothermal veining and stockworks formation; (IV) supergene placer formation. Transtension triggered adiabatic melting of metasomatized mantle, generating alkaline magmas that ascended along shear zones to form shallowly emplaced (2.6–8.7 km) anorogenic alkaline complexes. Fertile global analogues share this convergence of transtensional shearing, crustal assimilation, and F-rich fluid flux.

item.title: Radiogenic heat production in granite and its role in geothermal resource distribution and targeting
item.dc:creator: Yuhang Sun
Jinchuan Zhang
Xuan Tang
Xiaochao Tong
Qianchao Li
Lei Chen
Tiancheng Xie
Xingxu Zhao
Lixun Bai
Yue Zeng
item.description: Heat sources are fundamental to comprehending the origin of geothermal resources and are vital for the effective exploration and usage of these resources. Granitic heat production is a principal source of heat. However, its influence on the distribution of high-temperature geothermal systems and on target area selection is not fully understood. This study investigates the characteristics of granitic heat production and its influence on geothermal systems by utilizing rock data from the Global Whole-Rock Geochemical Database, integrating geothermal-geological analyses of the Western United States, Cathaysia Block, and Gangdise Belt regions, and incorporating assessments of typical geothermal wells globally. The subsequent findings are derived: (1) Intrusive rocks with higher acidity typically demonstrate increased heat production, while an excessively elevated SiO₂ content may diminish the concentration of radioactive elements. The global average heat generation of granite is estimated at roughly 3.177 ± 0.96 μW/m3, with thorium as the primary contributor, uranium as a moderate contributor, and potassium as the least contributor. (2) The global allocation of geothermal resources appropriate for power generation is strongly influenced by the presence of granite within Mesozoic–Cenozoic orogenic belts. Current geothermal power facilities are primarily situated near granitic formations with elevated heat generation. (3) Granites developed during stages of crustal thinning are more prone to demonstrate elevated heat generation. Anomalously elevated granitic heat production values are globally linked to supercontinent development and collision events. (4) Post-Mesozoic granites with an average radiogenic heat production exceeding the contemporary global granite average can function as efficient heat source rocks for geothermal systems. (5) Combinations of granite with various geological formations yield unique geothermal gradient curves. Reservoirs situated over hidden granite formations frequently constitute the most economically advantageous drilling targets. (6) Mantle heat movement influences the potential of high-temperature geothermal resources, whereas the distribution of high-heat-production granites directs the identification of advantageous geothermal zones and specific drilling targets. The synergistic integration of water supplies, heat sources, and fracture systems underpins the establishment of high-temperature hydrothermal geothermal systems. These discoveries offer significant references for international geothermal exploration and the identification of drilling prospects. Published: 2026-06-26T23:30:12+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.559 (consider)

item.title: What drove hinterland uplift and exhumation in the southern central Andes (29−34°S)?
item.dc:creator: Chelsea Mackaman-Lofland
Julie C. Fosdick
Richard A. Ketcham
Ana C. Lossada
María Pía Rodríguez
Vanesa D. Litvak
Macarena Bertoa del Llano
Julieta Suriano
José Mescua
Laura B. Giambiagi
Tomas N. Capaldi
Samuel G. Robbins
Daniel F. Stockli
Brian K. Horton
item.description: The elevated Andean hinterland at 29−34°S overlies a modern spatial transition from normal to flat-slab subduction. Development of this zone of high topography has been attributed to crustal thickening driven by internal (hinterland) shortening, underthrusting during shortening in external fold-thrust belts, or deeper processes associated with increased buoyancy forces and/or plate coupling above the flat slab. We integrate geologic and geo/thermochronological data to resolve the timing and magnitude of rock uplift, exhumation, and cooling necessary to evaluate possible mechanisms of hinterland construction. New and published apatite (U-Th)/He, apatite fission track, and zircon (U-Th)/He data and allied thermal history models from hinterland samples spanning >400 km along strike define protracted low-temperature residence of most samples since the late Mesozoic, followed by rapid Neogene cooling after ca. 20−15 Ma. Multi-sample models and other datasets demonstrate the influence of shortening-induced exhumation, with considerable spatial variations identified for different orogen-perpendicular (E-W) transects. At 29−31°S, ∼10° east-down tilting recorded hinterland development via continental underthrusting linked to external (eastern) fold-thrust belt shortening. In contrast, at 31−33°S, underthrusting was overprinted by internal (hinterland) shortening. At 33−34°S, reverse faults within the hinterland principally drove rock uplift and exhumation. These latitudinal variations in the kinematics and style of hinterland deformation coincide with along-strike changes in structural/stratigraphic inheritance, arc magmatism, and subduction geometry. Our findings underscore the range of processes governing orogenic construction above a modern type-example of flat-slab subduction, while demonstrating the enhanced capabilities of multi-sample thermal history modeling to test structural and geodynamic hypotheses. Published: 2026-06-29T19:34:27+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.554 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chelsea Mackaman-Lofland; Julie C. Fosdick; Richard A. Ketcham; Ana C. Lossada; María Pía Rodríguez; Vanesa D. Litvak; Macarena Bertoa del Llano; Julieta Suriano; José Mescua; Laura B. Giambiagi; Tomas N. Capaldi; Samuel G. Robbins; Daniel F. Stockli; Brian K. Horton

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b39076.1

Relevance: 0.554 (consider)

The elevated Andean hinterland at 29−34°S overlies a modern spatial transition from normal to flat-slab subduction. Development of this zone of high topography has been attributed to crustal thickening driven by internal (hinterland) shortening, underthrusting during shortening in external fold-thrust belts, or deeper processes associated with increased buoyancy forces and/or plate coupling above the flat slab. We integrate geologic and geo/thermochronological data to resolve the timing and magnitude of rock uplift, exhumation, and cooling necessary to evaluate possible mechanisms of hinterland construction. New and published apatite (U-Th)/He, apatite fission track, and zircon (U-Th)/He data and allied thermal history models from hinterland samples spanning >400 km along strike define protracted low-temperature residence of most samples since the late Mesozoic, followed by rapid Neogene cooling after ca. 20−15 Ma. Multi-sample models and other datasets demonstrate the influence of shortening-induced exhumation, with considerable spatial variations identified for different orogen-perpendicular (E-W) transects. At 29−31°S, ∼10° east-down tilting recorded hinterland development via continental underthrusting linked to external (eastern) fold-thrust belt shortening. In contrast, at 31−33°S, underthrusting was overprinted by internal (hinterland) shortening. At 33−34°S, reverse faults within the hinterland principally drove rock uplift and exhumation. These latitudinal variations in the kinematics and style of hinterland deformation coincide with along-strike changes in structural/stratigraphic inheritance, arc magmatism, and subduction geometry. Our findings underscore the range of processes governing orogenic construction above a modern type-example of flat-slab subduction, while demonstrating the enhanced capabilities of multi-sample thermal history modeling to test structural and geodynamic hypotheses.

item.title: Magnesium isotopes constrain connectivity and environmental resilience among ocean basins during the Early Triassic
item.dc:creator: Zhongya Hu
Weiqiang Li
Robert J. Newton
Sylvain Richoz
Yasufumi Iryu
Satoshi Takahashi
Takumi Maekawa
Zhiguang Xia
Shouye Yang
Shu-Zhong Shen
Hua Zhang
item.description: The pronounced spatial heterogeneity in the severity of the end-Permian mass extinction is suggested as the result of changes in oceanic connectivity, which regulate the buffering capacity of marine environments against global perturbations. However, direct geochemical constraints remain limited. Magnesium isotopes (δ26Mg) of seawater serve as a sensitive tracer for oceanic restriction. Here, we present reconstructed seawater δ26Mg records from the Neo-Tethys and Panthalassa oceans and compare them with existing data from the Paleo-Tethys. Our results reveal a fundamental divergence: seawater δ26Mg remained stable (−0.4‰ to −0.3‰) in the Neo-Tethys and Panthalassa across the Permian–Triassic transition, whereas it increased by more than 0.5‰ in the Paleo-Tethys. This contrast suggests that the Neo-Tethys was effectively connected to the vast, well-buffered Panthalassa Ocean, while the Paleo-Tethys was a more restricted basin. Mass balance modeling suggests that the open Panthalassa/Neo-Tethys system maintained a significantly larger Mg reservoir and greater capacity to buffer external changes, thereby supporting relatively stable environmental conditions. However, the restricted Paleo-Tethys is more sensitive to chemical disturbances, potentially leading to more pronounced environmental stress. Our Mg isotope data provide geochemical constraints on ocean connectivity and suggest that differences in buffering capacity may have played an important role in shaping the spatial heterogeneity of biotic catastrophe during the end-Permian mass extinction. The degree of restriction and inter-basin exchange in deep-water areas warrant further investigation. Published: 2026-06-25T07:52:44+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.552 (consider)
